text: Umbilical hernias are sometimes accompanied by an additional epigastric hernia, especially in patients with recti-divarication. One of the surgical techniques that can be used for a hernia repair in these cases is the preperitoneal placement of a synthetic mesh known as the TAP technique. This procedure allows to reinforce the abdominal wall around the defect with a large mesh that can be laid flat without the risk of its wrinkling and deformation. An additional significant benefit is the separation between the mesh and the intestines.
The surgeon carefully dissects the space between the peritoneum and the posterior rectus sheath, taking care due to the thin and fragile nature of the peritoneum.

The surgeon dissects in the preperitoneal space, reducing the hernia sac from the subcutaneous tissue while preserving the skin. Upon reaching the midline, the dissection reveals adipose tissue between the peritoneum and linea alba, facilitating the clearance of the hernia orifice and identification of additional defects.
The surgeon continues the dissection on the contralateral side, where the peritoneum appears thin and fragile, increasing the risk of creating small buttonholes.

The surgeon closes the hernia orifice tightly using a permanent continuous self-locking suture, applying multiple suture passes to distribute tension evenly. Measurements confirm sufficient space for mesh placement, with seven centimeters of overlap laterally from the midline on both sides for a three-centimeter hernia.
The surgeon inserts a self-fixating polyester mesh into the dissected preperitoneal space, utilizing the ample space to accommodate a 15 by 15 cm mesh without additional fixation.
The surgeon prepares to introduce a large mesh via an interior approach, indicating that separate approaches may be necessary for each hernia due to the complexity of the procedure.
